lessons.title: What is a magnetic field?
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## The magnetic field idea

A magnetic field is a region where moving charges or magnetic materials experience force. We represent it with the vector **B** and measure it in tesla (T).

Field lines show direction and relative strength:

- Arrows point from magnetic north to magnetic south outside a bar magnet
- Closer lines indicate stronger field
- Lines never cross

## Sources of magnetic fields

Magnetic fields are produced by permanent magnets, electric currents, and changing electric fields. At school level, the key link is that **current creates magnetism**.

lessons.title: Dipoles and Earth's magnetic field
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Magnetic dipoles

Every bar magnet has two poles. Isolated magnetic poles are not observed in normal classroom physics; cutting a magnet creates two smaller dipoles.

## Earth as a giant magnet

Earth's magnetic field acts approximately like a tilted dipole. A compass aligns with the local field direction, giving practical evidence of the field at each point.

Field strength and direction vary with latitude, which matters for charged particles in the magnetosphere and for navigation.

flashcards.back: A vector field where moving charges and magnetic dipoles experience force; measured in tesla.
SI unit of magnetic flux density, equivalent to N/(A*m).
Closer field lines represent stronger magnetic field magnitude.
A system with north and south poles, like a bar magnet or current loop.
A compass needle aligns tangent to local magnetic field lines.
Earth behaves approximately like a tilted magnetic dipole.

questions.prompt: The SI unit of magnetic field strength B is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic flux density B is measured in tesla (T).

questions.prompt: Where magnetic field lines are drawn closer together, the field is:
questions.explanation: Field-line density is a visual cue for field magnitude.

questions.prompt: A compass needle points along:
questions.explanation: The needle aligns with the magnetic field at its location.

questions.prompt: Cutting a bar magnet in half produces:
choices: A single north pole and a single south pole
Two smaller magnets each with north and south poles
questions.explanation: Magnetic dipoles persist; each piece becomes a dipole.

questions.prompt: Which is NOT a source of magnetic field?
questions.explanation: At this level, relevant sources are magnets and moving/ changing electric phenomena.

questions.prompt: Earth's field is commonly approximated as:
questions.explanation: Earth behaves approximately like a tilted dipole magnet.

lessons.title: Right-hand grip rule for a straight wire
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Circular fields around a wire

A straight current-carrying wire creates concentric circular magnetic field lines around the wire.

Use the right-hand grip rule:

- Thumb points in conventional current direction
- Curled fingers show magnetic field direction

The field magnitude near a long straight wire follows:

> B = mu0 I / (2 pi r)

So stronger current gives stronger field, while greater distance weakens the field.

lessons.title: Solenoids and electromagnets
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Solenoid behavior

A solenoid is a coil of wire. Inside a long solenoid, magnetic field lines are nearly parallel and field strength is approximately uniform.

> B = mu0 n I (ideal long solenoid)

where n is turns per meter.

## Practical electromagnets

Adding an iron core greatly increases field strength because the core has high magnetic permeability. This principle is used in relays, doorbells, motors, and MRI hardware.

flashcards.back: Thumb in current direction, fingers curl in magnetic field direction around the conductor.
Concentric circles centered on the wire.
B = mu0 I / (2 pi r) for a long straight wire.
A coil of wire that produces a near-uniform internal magnetic field when current flows.
B = mu0 n I, where n is turns per meter.
A ferromagnetic core increases field strength significantly.

questions.prompt: For a straight wire, doubling current I while keeping r constant makes B:
questions.explanation: B is directly proportional to I in B = mu0 I/(2 pi r).

questions.prompt: For a straight wire, doubling distance r while keeping I constant makes B:
questions.explanation: B is inversely proportional to r.

questions.prompt: The right-hand grip rule for a wire links current to:
questions.explanation: It gives the sense of circular B around the conductor.

questions.prompt: Inside a long ideal solenoid, the magnetic field is approximately:
questions.explanation: Long-solenoid approximation gives near-uniform internal B.

questions.prompt: Increasing turns per meter n in a solenoid generally:
questions.explanation: B = mu0 n I, so larger n increases field magnitude.

questions.prompt: Adding an iron core to a solenoid usually:
questions.explanation: Ferromagnetic material increases magnetic flux density in the coil.

lessons.title: Magnetic force on moving charges
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Lorentz magnetic force

A charge moving in a magnetic field experiences force:

> F = q v B sin(theta)

where theta is the angle between velocity and field.

The force is perpendicular to both velocity and field, so magnetic fields do no mechanical work on the particle; they change direction of motion, not speed (in ideal conditions).

lessons.title: Force on current-carrying conductors
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Motor effect

A wire of length L carrying current I in field B experiences force:

> F = B I L sin(theta)

Direction is found by Fleming's left-hand rule (motor rule). This force underlies electric motors, speakers, and galvanometers.

Parallel current-carrying wires also exert forces on each other: same current direction attracts, opposite direction repels.

flashcards.back: F = q v B sin(theta).
Perpendicular to both velocity and magnetic field vectors.
When velocity/current is perpendicular to B (theta = 90 degrees).
F = B I L sin(theta).
They attract each other.
They repel each other.

questions.prompt: A charged particle moves parallel to B. Magnetic force is:
questions.explanation: sin(0) = 0 so F = qvBsin(theta) gives zero force.

questions.prompt: For a fixed charge and speed, magnetic force is largest when theta equals:
questions.explanation: The sine term is maximum at 90 degrees.

questions.prompt: Magnetic force on a moving charge always acts:
questions.explanation: By cross-product direction, magnetic force is perpendicular to v and B.

questions.prompt: Force on a straight conductor in a field is given by:
questions.explanation: Current-carrying wire force follows F = BILsin(theta).

questions.prompt: Two parallel wires carry currents in the same direction. They:
questions.explanation: Same-direction currents attract due to their magnetic interaction.

questions.prompt: Because magnetic force is perpendicular to velocity, in ideal motion it changes:
questions.explanation: Perpendicular force changes direction of momentum without doing work on speed.

lessons.title: Faraday's law and changing flux
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Magnetic flux

Magnetic flux through a loop is:

> Phi = B A cos(theta)

Faraday's law states that induced emf is proportional to the rate of change of flux:

> emf = -N dPhi/dt

Changing B, area, orientation, or loop number can induce voltage.

lessons.title: Lenz's law and direction
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## The minus sign meaning

Lenz's law says induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux causing it.

If flux into the page increases, induced current creates a field out of the page. If flux decreases, induced current tries to reinforce the original direction.

This conservation-consistent opposition is essential in generators, braking systems, and induction cooktops.

flashcards.back: Phi = B A cos(theta), the amount of magnetic field passing through a surface.
emf = -N dPhi/dt. Induced emf depends on how quickly flux changes.
Induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux.
Change field strength, loop area, orientation angle, or move magnet/coil relative to each other.
Mechanical rotation changes flux through a coil, producing alternating emf.
Represents opposition to flux change, consistent with energy conservation.

questions.prompt: Induced emf appears when magnetic flux through a loop is:
questions.explanation: Faraday's law links emf to the rate of change of flux.

questions.prompt: If B doubles while area and angle are constant, flux Phi will:
questions.explanation: Flux is directly proportional to B in Phi = BAcos(theta).

questions.prompt: The minus sign in emf = -N dPhi/dt comes from:
questions.explanation: It encodes opposition to change in flux direction.

questions.prompt: Increasing the number of turns N in a coil generally makes induced emf:
questions.explanation: Induced emf is proportional to N.

questions.prompt: A magnet moving toward a coil causes induced current because:
choices: Magnetic flux through the coil changes
questions.explanation: Relative motion changes flux linkage, inducing emf.

questions.prompt: If inward flux through a loop is decreasing, the induced field tends to be:
questions.explanation: Lenz's law makes induced current reinforce inward flux to oppose its decrease.

lessons.title: Transformer ratios and power transfer
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Ideal transformer model

Transformers use mutual induction between primary and secondary coils:

> Vs/Vp = Ns/Np

For an ideal transformer, input and output power are approximately equal:

> Vp Ip approx Vs Is

Step-up transformers raise voltage and lower current for efficient long-distance transmission.

lessons.title: Motors, generators, and practical limits
lessons.bodyMarkdown:
## Motor vs generator

- **Motor**: electrical energy to mechanical rotation (uses force on current in B)
- **Generator**: mechanical rotation to electrical energy (uses changing flux)

## Non-ideal effects

Real systems lose energy due to resistance, heating, eddy currents, and hysteresis. Engineering design reduces losses using laminated cores, suitable materials, and cooling.

flashcards.back: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np for an ideal transformer.
Has more turns on the secondary; raises voltage and lowers current.
Has fewer turns on the secondary; lowers voltage and raises current.
For the same power, higher voltage means lower current and less I^2R loss.
Force on current-carrying conductors in magnetic fields creates torque.
Changing magnetic flux through coils induces emf and current.

questions.prompt: In an ideal transformer, if Ns > Np then output voltage is:
questions.explanation: From Vs/Vp = Ns/Np, greater secondary turns means higher secondary voltage.

questions.prompt: For fixed transmitted power, raising transmission voltage generally makes current:
questions.explanation: P = VI, so for constant P, larger V implies smaller I.

questions.prompt: Lower transmission current reduces line losses because resistive loss scales as:
questions.explanation: Joule heating in wires is P_loss = I^2R.

questions.prompt: A device that converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy is a:
questions.explanation: Motors use magnetic forces on current-carrying coils to produce torque.

questions.prompt: Which device relies directly on changing magnetic flux to produce emf?
questions.explanation: Generators use electromagnetic induction from rotating coils/fields.

questions.prompt: Which is a common source of transformer inefficiency?
questions.explanation: Real cores and windings produce thermal and magnetic losses.

glossary.definition: A vector field that exerts force on moving charges and magnetic dipoles.
SI unit of magnetic flux density.
A source with north and south poles, such as a bar magnet or current loop.
A rule for current direction and circular magnetic field direction around conductors.
A helical coil producing an approximately uniform internal magnetic field when current flows.
Magnetic force on a moving charge, F = qvBsin(theta).
Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
Phi = BAcos(theta), amount of field through a surface.
Induced emf is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage.
Induced current direction opposes the change in flux that caused it.
A device using mutual induction to change AC voltage levels.
Circulating currents induced in conductors that can cause unwanted heating losses.
